---
title: "quizdown for Quarto — MDS Extension"
filters:
  - quizdown
---

Interactive quizzes for Quarto documents, powered by [quizdown-js](https://github.com/bonartm/quizdown-js).
MDS fork of [parmsam/quarto-quizdown](https://github.com/parmsam/quarto-quizdown), extended with new question types and a print review mode.

**Install:**

```bash
quarto add UBC-MDS/quarto-quizdown-mds-ext
```

---

## Try it — full quiz

All five question types in one quiz. Answer everything, hit ✓✓ to evaluate, then **`Cmd/Ctrl+P`** to see the print review sheet.

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
---

# What is the output of this Python code?

~~~python
x = [2, 3, 4]
x[2] = 9
print(x[2])
~~~

> Python lists are **zero-indexed** and **mutable** — `x[2]` is the third element.

1. [x] 9
    > Correct — `x[2] = 9` overwrites the third element before `print` runs.
1. [ ] 4
    > That was the original value at index 2 before the assignment.
1. [ ] 2
    > 2 is the value at index 0.
1. [ ] 3
    > 3 is the value at index 1.

## Which of the following are supervised learning tasks?

Select **all** that apply.

> In **supervised learning** the model learns from labeled examples — training data where the correct output (target) is already known.

- [x] Predicting house prices from features like size and location
    > ✓ Supervised — the training data includes known sale prices (continuous target).
- [ ] Grouping customers by purchasing behaviour with no labels
    > ✗ This is **clustering** — an unsupervised method with no target variable.
- [x] Classifying email as spam or not spam
    > ✓ Supervised — human-labelled examples (spam / not spam) train the model.
- [x] Forecasting tomorrow's temperature from historical weather data
    > ✓ Supervised — past temperature measurements are the labels.
- [ ] Discovering latent topics in a corpus of documents
    > ✗ Topic modelling (e.g. LDA) is unsupervised — no labels provided.

## Put the supervised ML workflow steps in order!

> A typical supervised ML project follows this sequence — getting the order wrong is a common source of data leakage and overfitting bugs.

1. Define the problem and success metric
2. Collect and clean data
3. Exploratory data analysis
4. Feature engineering
5. Split into train / validation / test sets
6. Train and tune a model
7. Evaluate on the held-out test set
8. Deploy and monitor

## Match each Python data structure to its key property!

- list :: Ordered, mutable sequence
    > Lists maintain insertion order and can be modified after creation — the most general-purpose container.
- tuple :: Ordered, immutable sequence
    > Tuples are like lists but frozen — useful for dictionary keys and function return values.
- dict :: Key-value store with unique keys
    > Dictionaries map unique keys to values; insertion order is preserved in Python 3.7+.
- set :: Unordered collection of unique values
    > Sets deduplicate automatically — ideal for membership tests and set operations.
- :: Linked list
    > Python has no built-in linked list, though `collections.deque` provides O(1) head/tail operations.

> Think about **mutability** and **ordering** — those two properties distinguish all four types.

## Classify each ML task as Regression or Classification!

The chips **Regression** and **Classification** are reusable — drag or click each to as many rows as needed.

> **Regression** predicts a *continuous* value (a number on a scale). **Classification** predicts a *category* (one of a finite set of classes).

- Predict house price :: Regression
    > The output is a dollar amount — a continuous value.
- Detect spam email :: Classification
    > The output is spam / not spam — a binary category.
- Forecast next month's sales revenue :: Regression
    > Revenue is continuous — even though it's rounded to cents.
- Identify the digit in a handwritten image :: Classification
    > The output is one of 10 discrete classes (0–9).
- Estimate a patient's remaining hospital stay in days :: Regression
    > Days is a continuous (or at least ordinal) quantity.
- Diagnose whether a tumour is malignant or benign :: Classification
    > A binary categorical outcome.
- :: Clustering
    > Clustering is **unsupervised** — no target label is predicted.
```

---

## Matching and classification questions

### 1-to-1 matching

Each right-side chip belongs to exactly one left prompt. Chips are consumed from the pool when placed. Distractors (`:: Value`) are chips that don't match any prompt.

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
---

## Match each Python data structure to its key property!

- list :: Ordered, mutable sequence
    > Lists maintain insertion order and can be modified in place.
- tuple :: Ordered, immutable sequence
    > Like a list but frozen — safe to use as a dictionary key.
- dict :: Key-value store with unique keys
    > Maps unique keys to values; O(1) average lookup.
- set :: Unordered collection of unique values
    > Deduplicates automatically; O(1) membership test.
- :: Linked list
    > Python has no built-in linked list.

> Think about mutability and ordering.
```

### Multi-match / classification

When the **same right-side label appears more than once**, it becomes a **reusable chip** that stays in the pool after being placed — perfect for classify-into-bins tasks.

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
---

## Classify each ML task as Regression or Classification!

> Regression → continuous target. Classification → categorical target.

- Predict house price :: Regression
    > The target (price) is a continuous number.
- Detect spam email :: Classification
    > The target is a category: spam or not spam.
- Forecast stock return :: Regression
    > Returns are continuous values, not categories.
- Identify handwritten digit :: Classification
    > Digits 0–9 are discrete categories.
- Estimate delivery time :: Regression
    > Delivery time is a continuous quantity.
- :: Clustering
    > Clustering is unsupervised — there is no target label to predict.
```

---

## Multiple choice and single choice

### Single choice

Exactly one correct answer. Use a **numbered list** (`1.`) with `[x]` on the correct option.

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
---

# What does `df.shape` return in pandas?

> `shape` is an attribute, not a method — no parentheses needed.

1. [x] A tuple `(rows, columns)`
    > Correct — e.g. `(150, 5)` for a DataFrame with 150 rows and 5 columns.
1. [ ] The number of rows only
    > `len(df)` gives the number of rows.
1. [ ] A list of column names
    > `df.columns` gives column names.
1. [ ] The total number of cells
    > `df.size` gives rows × columns.
```

### Multiple choice

One or more correct answers. Use an **unordered list** (`-`) with `[x]` on all correct options.

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
---

## Which of the following are Python mutable types?

> A **mutable** object can be changed after it is created. An **immutable** object cannot.

- [x] list
    > ✓ Lists can be modified — you can append, remove, and reassign elements.
- [ ] tuple
    > ✗ Tuples are immutable — their contents cannot be changed after creation.
- [x] dict
    > ✓ Dictionaries are mutable — keys and values can be added, changed, or removed.
- [ ] str
    > ✗ Strings are immutable in Python — operations like `.upper()` return a new string.
- [x] set
    > ✓ Sets are mutable — you can add and discard elements.
- [ ] frozenset
    > ✗ `frozenset` is the immutable counterpart of `set`.
```

---

## Sequence

Students drag items into the correct order. Use a **numbered list without checkboxes**.

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
---

## Put the Git workflow steps in order!

> This is the standard cycle for contributing a change to a shared repository.

1. Create a feature branch from `main`
2. Make and stage your changes (`git add`)
3. Commit with a descriptive message (`git commit`)
4. Push the branch to the remote (`git push`)
5. Open a pull request
6. Address review feedback
7. Merge into `main`
8. Delete the feature branch
```

---

## Print review mode

After a student answers all questions and clicks **✓✓ evaluate**, pressing **`Cmd/Ctrl+P`** replaces the interactive quiz with a static review sheet showing:

- Every question with its **student-selected answers**
- The **correct answer(s)** highlighted
- **Per-option / per-pair feedback** written into the question
- **Pass / needs review** status per question

No navigation needed — all questions appear at once. This makes printed quizzes useful as study notes.

::: {.callout-tip}
**Try it:** go back to the [full quiz](#try-it-full-quiz) at the top, answer all five questions, click ✓✓, then press `Cmd/Ctrl+P`. The print dialog will show the full review sheet.
:::

---

## Embedding quizzes in your pages

### Basic usage

Add the filter to your document front matter, then write a quiz inside a ` ```quizdown ` code block:

````markdown
---
filters:
  - quizdown
---

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
primary_color: "#4b75a8"
---

## Question heading

Answer options here.
```
````

### Quiz inside a "Check your understanding" callout

You can embed a quiz directly inside a Quarto callout — students expand it when they're ready:

````markdown
::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="✏️ Check your understanding"}

```quizdown
## Which of these is a supervised learning task?

1. [ ] Clustering customers by behaviour
1. [x] Predicting whether a loan will default
    > Correct — the model is trained on historical loan outcomes (labels).
1. [ ] Dimensionality reduction with PCA
```

:::
````

**Live example:**

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="✏️ Check your understanding"}

```quizdown
---
shuffle_answers: true
---

## Which of these is a supervised learning task?

> Supervised learning requires labelled training data — examples where the correct output is already known.

1. [ ] Clustering customers by purchase behaviour
    > No — clustering is unsupervised. There is no target label.
1. [x] Predicting whether a loan will default
    > Yes — the model is trained on historical loan outcomes (default / no default) as labels.
1. [ ] Reducing 100 features to 2 dimensions with PCA
    > No — PCA is unsupervised dimensionality reduction.
1. [ ] Grouping news articles by topic with LDA
    > No — LDA topic modelling is unsupervised.
```

:::

### Quiz configuration options

Pass YAML front matter inside the quiz block to configure it:

```yaml
---
primary_color: "#4b75a8"   # accent colour (hex or CSS name)
secondary_color: lightgray  # chip / background colour
text_color: black
shuffle_questions: false    # randomise question order
shuffle_answers: true       # randomise answer/chip order
---
```

---

## How it works — the syntax

A single ` ```quizdown ` block can hold multiple questions. Question type is inferred from the list syntax.

| Question type | List syntax | Correct marker |
|---|---|---|
| Single choice | Ordered list `1.` | `[x]` on one item |
| Multiple choice | Unordered list `-` | `[x]` on one or more items |
| Sequence | Ordered list `1.` | No `[x]` — order is the answer |
| Matching / classification | Unordered list `-` | `Left :: Right` pairs |

### Feedback and hints

```markdown
## Question heading

Optional overall hint as a blockquote.

> Hint text shown when student clicks the lightbulb 💡.

- [x] Correct option
    > Per-option feedback — shown after evaluation.
- [ ] Wrong option
    > Explain why this is wrong.
```

For matching questions:

```markdown
- Left prompt :: Right chip
    > Per-pair feedback shown after evaluation.
- :: Distractor chip
    > Shown when this distractor is placed in a slot.
```

---

## Attributions

- Original Quarto extension: [parmsam/quarto-quizdown](https://github.com/parmsam/quarto-quizdown) (MIT, Sam Parmar 2025)
- quizdown-js library: [bonartm/quizdown-js](https://github.com/bonartm/quizdown-js) (MIT, Malte Bonart 2020–21)
